text: Formerly, I, Kwang Kâu, dreamt that I was a butterfly, a butterfly flying about, feeling that it was enjoying itself I did not know that it was Kâu. Suddenly I awoke, and was myself again, the veritable Kâu. I did not know whether it had formerly been Kâu dreaming that he was a butterfly, or it was now a butterfly dreaming that it was Kâu.
author: Zhuangzi
source: Internet Sacred Text Archive (SBE v39, Legge), via Wayback Machine snapshot
out_pulls_note: High heat: the closing 'I did not know whether...' lands as a genuine vertigo a stranger feels in the body, no name required.
search_notes: James Legge, SBE v39, 1891 ('The Writings of Kwang-dze'). No clean Legge plain text exists on Gutenberg (only Giles 1889) and all three archive.org SBE-v39 OCR scans garble the proper name 'Kwang Kau' into 'Awang Au / Adu / Kau / TTau', which would fail byte-match. I therefore used the byte-clean sacred-texts.com HTML via the Wayback Machine (sacred-texts itself is Cloudflare-blocked to curl). Verbatim substring confirmed after normalising whitespace and curly quotes; the span deliberately stops before Legge's footnote superscripts so none are included. NOTE: the source has a known OCR slip 'enjoying itself I did not know' (Legge's print has a period after 'itself'); kept verbatim, not silently fixed. Confidence high on the famous Bk II passage.
